stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 41+3 weeks is admitted for induction of labour for prolonged pregnancy. She is well and has had normal antenatal care. Her membranes are intact. The fetal heart rate is reassuring on CTG. She declines artificial rupture of membranes today and is keen for the least invasive starting option.

data_table.Examination: Cervix posterior, firm, 1 cm long, 1 cm dilated, vertex at station -3, Bishop's score 3
data_table.Investigations: CTG reassuring, deepest pool of liquor 4 cm, estimated fetal weight 3.4 kg on growth scan 2 weeks ago

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate initial method of induction?

options.text: Artificial rupture of membranes immediately followed by an oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: ARM followed by oxytocin is appropriate when the cervix is favourable (Bishop's score 7 or more) or after successful ripening; with Bishop's 3 it is likely to fail and the woman has declined ARM. (Source: RANZCOG IOL)

options.text: Mechanical cervical ripening using a transcervical balloon catheter overnight
options.rationale: For an unfavourable cervix (Bishop's score 6 or less) in a woman preferring a less pharmacological approach, mechanical ripening with a balloon catheter is an Australian first-line option with low hyperstimulation risk. (Source: RANZCOG IOL)

options.text: Vaginal misoprostol 25 micrograms every 4 hours up to a maximum of 6 doses
options.rationale: Vaginal misoprostol is effective for ripening but carries a higher hyperstimulation risk and is restricted in Australian protocols to specific scenarios; balloon catheter is a lower-risk first step in many AU units. (Source: RANZCOG IOL)

options.text: Outpatient weekly membrane sweeping until spontaneous onset of labour
options.rationale: Membrane sweeping is offered antenatally to reduce IOL likelihood, not as an in-hospital induction method when admission has already occurred for IOL. (Source: RANZCOG IOL)

options.text: Elective caesarean section under spinal anaesthesia scheduled tomorrow morning
options.rationale: Caesarean is not first-line for prolonged pregnancy without obstetric indication; the woman is a candidate for induction and has not requested caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG IOL)

explanation.summary: The Bishop's score combines cervical dilation, effacement, station, position and consistency. A score of 6 or less is unfavourable and benefits from cervical ripening before oxytocin and ARM. Options include mechanical ripening (Cook or Foley balloon) and pharmacological agents (dinoprostone gel or pessary, misoprostol where protocols allow). Mechanical methods have a lower hyperstimulation rate. A favourable cervix (7 or more) is managed with ARM and oxytocin. IOL after 41+0 reduces stillbirth risk in prolonged pregnancy.
key_points: Bishop's score 6 or less = unfavourable cervix, ripen first
Balloon catheter is a low-hyperstimulation Australian first-line ripening option
ARM and oxytocin follow ripening or are used when cervix is already favourable
explanation.pearls: Transcervical balloon catheters are typically inflated to 40 to 60 mL and left in place for up to 12 hours or until they fall out, achieving a mean Bishop's score increase of around 3 points.

stem: A 15-year-old girl is reviewed by her general practitioner with her mother. Over the past 4 months she has had persistent low mood, anhedonia, fatigue, mid-afternoon naps, dropping school grades, and irritability. She denies suicidal ideation. She has tried 8 weeks of psychological therapy with no improvement. There is no substance use. Her mother is keen to consider medication.

data_table.Examination: Affect dysphoric, mood subjectively low, reactive, no psychomotor retardation, insight preserved, no psychotic features, MSE otherwise normal
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TFTs, B12, vitamin D, urine drug screen all normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate pharmacological first step?

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily, titrated upward as clinically tolerated
options.rationale: Sertraline is a reasonable second-line SSRI in adolescent depression but does not have the same level of trial evidence as fluoxetine, which is the only SSRI approved for under-18s in Australia. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood Disorders)

options.text: Mirtazapine 15 mg orally at night to address sleep difficulty and appetite
options.rationale: Mirtazapine is not first-line in adolescent depression; its sedating and weight-gain effects are not preferred, and SSRIs have stronger paediatric evidence. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood Disorders)

options.text: Fluoxetine 10 mg orally daily with weekly review during the first 4 weeks
options.rationale: Fluoxetine is the first-line SSRI in adolescent moderate-severe depression with the strongest efficacy evidence and is the only SSRI specifically approved in this age group in Australia. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood Disorders)

options.text: Venlafaxine 75 mg orally daily as a sustained-release titration schedule
options.rationale: SNRIs are not first-line in adolescent depression and carry a higher discontinuation syndrome and blood-pressure burden; SSRIs are preferred initially. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood Disorders)

options.text: Amitriptyline 25 mg orally at night for low mood and insomnia management
options.rationale: Tricyclic antidepressants have not shown efficacy in adolescent depression and carry significant overdose risk, so are not recommended in this age group. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood Disorders)

explanation.summary: Australian guidance for moderate-severe adolescent depression that has not responded to 6 to 8 weeks of psychological therapy recommends fluoxetine as the first-line SSRI, with weekly initial reviews because of a small absolute increase in suicidal ideation in the first month of treatment. Sertraline and escitalopram are reasonable second-line SSRIs if fluoxetine is not tolerated. Combining medication with continued CBT or interpersonal therapy improves response. TCAs and MAOIs are not used in adolescents.
key_points: Fluoxetine is the only SSRI specifically approved in Australian adolescents
Combine pharmacotherapy with continued psychological therapy
Weekly review in the first month to monitor suicidal ideation and side effects
explanation.pearls: Black-box warnings for suicidal ideation with SSRIs in under-25s reflect a small absolute risk increase confined mostly to the first 1 to 4 weeks; weekly review during that window mitigates the risk.

stem: A 36-year-old paramedic presents 4 months after a critical-incident motor vehicle collision in which a colleague died. He describes intrusive memories, nightmares, hypervigilance, avoidance of the highway, and emotional numbing. Symptoms are present most days. He has had no previous mental illness, no substance use, and is currently on leave from work. There is no suicidal ideation. He prefers a non-pharmacological starting point.

data_table.Examination: Affect anxious, mood subjectively low, sleep poor with nightmares 4 nights/week, no psychotic features, insight preserved

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate first-line therapy?

options.text: Supportive counselling without trauma-focused techniques over 12 weeks
options.rationale: Generic supportive counselling without trauma-focused techniques is inferior to trauma-focused CBT or EMDR for PTSD symptom reduction. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines 2020)

options.text: Critical incident stress debriefing delivered as a single-session intervention
options.rationale: Single-session psychological debriefing is not effective for PTSD prevention or treatment and may worsen outcomes; it is no longer recommended. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines 2020)

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg orally daily as first-line pharmacological monotherapy
options.rationale: Sertraline has evidence in PTSD but Australian guidance positions it as second-line after trauma-focused psychological therapy in patients who can engage with therapy, and the patient prefers a non-pharmacological start. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines 2020)

options.text: Trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy weekly for 8 to 12 sessions
options.rationale: Trauma-focused CBT is a Phoenix Australia and RANZCP first-line recommendation for adult PTSD, with consistent strong evidence across military and civilian populations. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines 2020)

options.text: Prazosin 1 mg orally at night targeting trauma nightmares as sole therapy
options.rationale: Prazosin is an adjunct for trauma-related nightmares but is not a stand-alone PTSD treatment; primary therapy is trauma-focused psychological intervention. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines 2020)

explanation.summary: PTSD requires symptoms for at least 1 month after a Criterion A traumatic exposure, with intrusion, avoidance, negative cognitions/mood and hyperarousal clusters. Australian Phoenix guidelines and RANZCP recommend trauma-focused CBT or EMDR as first-line treatment for adult PTSD. SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) and venlafaxine are second-line, used when psychological therapy is unavailable, declined, or insufficient. Prazosin is an adjunct for nightmares. Critical incident debriefing is no longer recommended.
key_points: Trauma-focused CBT and EMDR are first-line for adult PTSD
Single-session debriefing is not recommended and may worsen outcomes
explanation.pearls: Phoenix Australia recommends 8 to 12 weekly sessions of trauma-focused CBT for adult PTSD; a typical course incorporates psychoeducation, in-vivo and imaginal exposure, and cognitive restructuring.

stem: A 52-year-old man with bipolar I disorder has been on lithium 750 mg at night for 6 years. Two days ago he started ramipril 5 mg for newly diagnosed hypertension. Today he attends with 24 hours of coarse hand tremor, nausea, vomiting and ataxia. He has been struggling to keep down fluids. His last lithium level 3 weeks ago was 0.7 mmol/L.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.0 degrees Celsius, HR 88, BP 130/82 mmHg, RR 16
data_table.Examination: Coarse tremor of both hands, mild ataxia on standing, normal mental status, mild dehydration, no nystagmus, deep tendon reflexes brisk
data_table.Investigations: Lithium level pending, Na 138, K 4.2, urea 8.4 mmol/L, creatinine 132 micromol/L (baseline 92)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer oral activated charcoal 50 g to bind any further absorbed lithium
options.rationale: Activated charcoal does not bind lithium meaningfully and is not used in lithium toxicity. (Source: AMH: Lithium)

options.text: Continue lithium and administer ondansetron 4 mg intravenously for nausea
options.rationale: Continuing lithium in a symptomatic patient with rising creatinine and a likely toxic level worsens neurological injury; the precipitant must be withdrawn. (Source: AMH: Lithium)

options.text: Arrange urgent haemodialysis now without waiting for the lithium level result
options.rationale: Haemodialysis is indicated for severe lithium toxicity (level above 4 mmol/L, level 2.5 to 4 with severe symptoms or renal failure, or impaired clearance) but is not first-line for mild-moderate toxicity that may respond to fluid resuscitation. (Source: AMH: Lithium)

options.text: Switch the antihypertensive to a thiazide diuretic and continue the lithium
options.rationale: Thiazides also raise lithium levels by reducing distal tubular sodium delivery; switching to a thiazide worsens lithium retention rather than helping it. (Source: AMH: Lithium)

options.text: Hold lithium and give intravenous normal saline at 1 L per hour and recheck
options.rationale: Acute on chronic lithium toxicity from ACE-inhibitor-induced renal lithium retention and dehydration is treated with stopping the precipitant, lithium hold and IV crystalloid to enhance renal excretion. (Source: AMH: Lithium)

explanation.summary: Acute on chronic lithium toxicity classically follows the addition of an ACE inhibitor, ARB, thiazide or NSAID to a chronic lithium user. Renal lithium retention plus dehydration tips levels into the toxic range. Early features are coarse tremor, GI upset, ataxia and confusion; severe toxicity includes seizures, coma and arrhythmia. Initial management is to stop the precipitant, hold lithium, give intravenous crystalloid, and check lithium and renal function. Haemodialysis is reserved for severe toxicity (level above 4 mmol/L, severe symptoms, renal failure).
key_points: ACEi, ARB, thiazide and NSAID raise lithium levels
Early features: tremor, GI symptoms, ataxia
Haemodialysis if level above 4 mmol/L or severe symptoms with renal impairment
explanation.pearls: Therapeutic lithium range is 0.4 to 1.0 mmol/L for maintenance and up to 1.2 mmol/L for acute mania; symptoms generally appear above 1.5 mmol/L and dialysis is considered above 4 mmol/L or above 2.5 mmol/L with renal failure.

stem: A 19-year-old woman with severe anorexia nervosa (BMI 13.5) is admitted to a medical ward for refeeding. Day 2 of structured refeeding at 30 kcal/kg/day, she develops new-onset peripheral oedema, calf cramps and palpitations. She is conscious and oriented. Her electrolytes were normal on admission.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.4 degrees Celsius, HR 110, BP 100/64 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 98% in room air
data_table.Examination: Bilateral pitting ankle oedema, calf tenderness on stretch, normal heart sounds, no peripheral neuropathy, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Phosphate 0.42 mmol/L (1.05 on admission), magnesium 0.58 mmol/L, potassium 3.1, glucose 4.4, ECG shows QTc 480 ms with no arrhythmia

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Replace phosphate, magnesium and potassium intravenously and reduce calories
options.rationale: This is biochemical refeeding syndrome with hypophosphataemia, hypomagnesaemia and hypokalaemia; standard practice is IV replacement of the three electrolytes plus thiamine, with reduced (not stopped) caloric load. (Source: ANZAED Guidelines)

options.text: Stop nutrition entirely until serum phosphate is above 1.0 mmol/L overnight
options.rationale: Stopping nutrition risks further metabolic collapse and reverses the gains of feeding; the correct response is to reduce caloric load and aggressively replace electrolytes, not zero feeding. (Source: ANZAED Guidelines)

options.text: Administer intravenous furosemide 40 mg for the new bilateral peripheral oedema
options.rationale: Refeeding oedema is from sodium and water retention plus reduced oncotic pressure; loop diuretics worsen electrolyte derangements and are contraindicated in this context. (Source: ANZAED Guidelines)

options.text: Increase the caloric load to compensate for any ongoing weight loss observed
options.rationale: Increasing calories during active refeeding syndrome will accelerate metabolic complications; the correct move is to reduce caloric load while replacing electrolytes. (Source: ANZAED Guidelines)

options.text: Commence a beta blocker for the tachycardia with serial QTc monitoring
options.rationale: Beta blockers in severe anorexia carry hypotension and bradycardia risks and do not address the underlying electrolyte problem driving the tachycardia and QT prolongation. (Source: ANZAED Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome is the rapid intracellular shift of phosphate, potassium and magnesium that follows reintroduction of carbohydrate in severely malnourished patients, classically those with BMI under 14 or rapid weight loss. Manifestations include hypophosphataemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypokalaemia, thiamine deficiency (Wernicke), peripheral oedema, cardiac arrhythmia and acute heart failure. Management is electrolyte replacement (intravenous if severe), thiamine 100 to 200 mg daily, reduction in caloric load (not cessation), and twice-daily biochemistry monitoring during the high-risk window of the first week.
key_points: Hypophosphataemia is the hallmark electrolyte derangement
Replace phosphate, magnesium, potassium and give thiamine; reduce calories, do not stop
Twice-daily electrolytes for the first 5 to 7 days of refeeding in high-risk patients
explanation.pearls: ANZAED guidance recommends starting refeeding at 20 to 30 kcal/kg/day with twice-daily electrolytes and pre-emptive thiamine 100 mg, increasing calories cautiously by 10 kcal/kg/day if biochemistry stays stable.
